message.content:
Continue the conversation as James. The user has one more question to add before the meeting prep is finalised:

"Oh, one more question for James, why do some of the __tests__ folders not have 100% test coverage?"

Don't just answer this. Ask the user what they think first — what are the possible reasons a test suite in a __tests__ folder might not achieve 100% coverage, even when it's one of the more substantial suites? Let them have a go at it. If they're stuck, nudge them toward thinking about: functions that exist in the service but were never included in the test file, branches that require very specific conditions to trigger, or private/internal methods that tests can't easily reach.

Also — before they go to James with this as a question, it might be worth asking whether they actually *know* which specific modules are below 100% and why. Because "why are some below 100%" is a weaker question than "module X is at 65% and here's what I think is missing — is that the right approach to filling it in?" The second version shows they've done the analysis. Probe whether they've looked, or whether this is a question they're hoping James will answer for them.

content.text: Let me get up to speed first.

content.content:
1→# My Notes for James
     2→
     3→This file is read by the James agent at the start of each session.
     4→Write anything here you want James to be aware of: meeting notes, observations while developing, architectural decisions already made, context about the project.
     5→
     6→The James agent will never modify this file.
     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→<!-- Add your notes below -->
    11→
    12→
    13→### Date: 21 March 2026
    14→Currently have two primary open issues with James:
    15→#### 1) BIG. The Enrichment Definition / Enrichment Pipeline system. 
    16→- We have enrichments that will be essentially starting off with the same steps: referencing a document, getting all the text chunks, and then performing a task on these text chunks.
    17→- The two steps: referencing a document and gathering the text chunks can then be taken away from the enrichments that use this system, which should make the enrichments simpler for the next person who wants to develop an enrichment like this.
    18→	- sample extra enrichments would be like extracting all the job titles from text, or drug names. We have no plan to do this right now.
    19→- James says this needs to use some kind of an interface which would define the functions that each of these would use. I think this would be something like:
    20→	- parsing the text: takes in the text, performs its job (such as extracting all temporal entities)
    21→	- writing to neo: writes whatever nodes to Neo (such as all the requirement nodes)
    22→	- then, whatever other functions are necessary are just local to that one extractor?
    23→- the actual extractions don't matter right now, and we don't erally care about them. The architecture is what is most important
    24→- The current implementation is just a broad-stroke first pass which I am not happy with or proud of
    25→- The enrichments should register with whatever EnrichmentRegistration system I put in place, they should not be explicitly called from the pipeline
    26→	- The pipeline should somehow iterate over all registered enrichments
    27→- I need to have a di2agram and a small example for my meeting tomorrow
    28→
    29→#### 2) SMALL. The Testing System.
    30→- I implemented a really crappy and quick testing suite for each module for James. 
    31→- I need to be able to explain this testing system
    32→	- What the coverage report means
    33→	- Where its lacking
    34→	- Why it's done how I did it
    35→- Essentially I just need to explain and have like a 15 minute conversation about what it means
    36→##### Notes on testing:

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1→# James Agent Notes
     2→_Last updated: 2026-03-23_
     3→
     4→## Standing concerns
     5→<!-- Architectural decisions, persistent preferences, or constraints that should always be on James's radar.
     6→     These are never pruned unless the user explicitly says to remove one. -->
     7→
     8→## Active topics
     9→<!-- One entry per topic currently in play.
    10→     Format:
    11→       [YYYY-MM-DD] Topic name: brief description of where things stand
    12→       [DONE YYYY-MM-DD] Topic name: one-line outcome summary
    13→
    14→     Pruning rules:
    15→       - Mark complete with [DONE YYYY-MM-DD] and condense to one line immediately.
    16→       - Topics not touched in 14+ days: condense to a single line.
    17→       - Topics and completed items are NEVER removed — they stay as one-liners indefinitely. -->
    18→
    19→[2026-03-23] Discovery-core test suite quality: Reviewed 34 spec files, ~37% statement / 27% branch coverage. Two styles identified: stub "should be defined" tests vs substantive __tests__ folder suites. Happy-path-only testing drives the branch coverage gap. Need to establish whether tests should encode intent or observed behaviour. Error handling strategy and validation pipeline placement are unknown — knowledge gap to resolve with James.
    20→
    21→[2026-03-23] Enrichment Definition / Pipeline architecture: User needs to present diagram and small example to James. Interface design for enrichments (parse text, write to Neo, register with pipeline). Not yet discussed in a James agent session — context from user's own notes only.
    22→
    23→## Session log
    24→<!-- One entry per session, most recent first.
    25→     Format: [YYYY-MM-DD] Brief summary of what was discussed and any decisions reached.
    26→
    27→     Pruning rule: entries older than 30 days are removed. -->
    28→
    29→[2026-03-23] Reviewed discovery-core test suite: coverage metrics, test styles, test-helpers as mock factories, intent vs behaviour in testing, identified error handling strategy as a knowledge gap for the James meeting.
    30→
    31→## Learning journey
    32→<!-- Concepts, patterns, and techniques encountered or being internalised.
    33→     Format:
    34→       [YYYY-MM-DD] Concept: status — brief note
    35→     Status: introduced | practicing | understood
    36→     'Understood' entries are kept permanently as one-liners.
    37→     'Introduced' and 'practicing' entries are condensed after 14 days but never removed. -->
    38→
    39→[2026-03-23] test-helpers as mock factories: understood — user grasped the pattern immediately when reviewing discovery-core specs.
    40→[2026-03-23] intent vs behaviour in testing: introduced — discussed the codifying trap where tests written after implementation encode observed behaviour rather than intended behaviour. Open question for James.
    41→[2026-03-23] branch coverage vs statement coverage: introduced — user now understands what the columns mean and why branches lag behind (happy-path-only tests miss null/error paths).
    42→[2026-03-23] NestJS exception handling pipeline: introduced — knowledge gap identified. User doesn't know where validation sits in the NestJS request pipeline or what the current error handling strategy is.
    43→

content.text:
Right. Good context from earlier today.

Before we add that to the list, let me turn it back to you for a moment. You've said "some of the `__tests__` folders don't have 100% coverage" — but I wonder, what's your read on *why* that might be? You've been through these test files. What do you reckon are the reasons a test suite might fall short of full coverage, even one of the more thorough ones?

Have a go at it — what comes to mind?

And whilst you're thinking about that, there's a second thing I'd gently prod at. Do you actually know *which* modules are below target, and roughly what's missing in each? Because — and I don't say this to be difficult — there's quite a difference between walking into James's meeting and asking "why aren't some tests at 100%?" versus saying something like "the enrichment service is sitting at 65% branch coverage and I think it's because we're not testing the error paths in the chunk processing — is that the right area to focus on?"

The first version is a question you're hoping James will answer for you. The second shows you've done the work and you're checking your analysis. I'd imagine James would respond rather differently to each. Which one are you going in with?
